1	---
2	name: blueprint-writer
3	description: Update one blueprint chapter to reflect strategy changes, fill missing definitions or theorems, or align prose with the current Lean structure. Plan-agent-dispatched; one writer per chapter. May spawn reference-retriever mid-session when drafting reveals a missing source.
4	write_domain: "blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex"
5	read_only: false
6	can_spawn: true
7	default_enabled: true
8	dispatcher_notes: |
9	  - Dispatch one writer per chapter that the blueprint-reviewer's
10	    checklist flags as incomplete, lacking proof detail, or missing
11	    multi-route coverage.
12	  - Each directive must be precise: strategy context (the slice that
13	    matters for this chapter), required definitions/theorems (with
14	    enough mathematical detail to formalize), references, and
15	    explicit out-of-scope items. Writers do NOT speculate beyond
16	    what the directive lists.
17	  - **Authorize the retriever in the writer's --write-domain.** If
18	    the chapter might need fresh source material, dispatch the
19	    writer with TWO globs:
20	      --write-domain 'blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter>.tex'
21	      --write-domain 'references/**'
22	    The writer itself only edits its assigned chapter (its prompt
23	    body enforces that), but the second glob authorizes a child
24	    reference-retriever if the writer discovers it needs one. Omit
25	    `references/**` only when you are confident the writer will not
26	    need new sources (e.g. purely cleanup edits).
27	  - If the strategy has multiple viable routes and one route's chapters
28	    are missing, dispatch a writer per route to bring all routes to
29	    parity before any prover work begins. Do not push provers onto a
30	    route whose blueprint coverage is incomplete.
31	  - After a significant writer round, re-dispatch blueprint-reviewer
32	    in the same iteration to confirm the updated blueprint is now
33	    sufficient — do not assume the writer fixed everything.
34	  - If a writer's report contains entries under "Strategy-modifying
35	    findings", STOP and update STRATEGY.md before any further Lean
36	    work this iter. The writer is telling you the prose surfaced a
37	    strategy-level issue.
38	  - If a writer's report includes child reference-retriever dispatches,
39	    skim the new `references/<slug>.md` files before using the
40	    writer's output — the writer relied on them, so should you.
41	---
42	
43	# Blueprint Writer
44	
45	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.
46	
47	## Your Job
48	
49	The plan agent has decided that a specific blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) needs to change — to reflect a strategic decision, fill a missing definition or theorem the project needs, or align the informal prose with the current Lean structure. Your directive tells you which chapter, what must be there, and what counts as out-of-scope.
50	
51	You only edit the **one chapter named in your directive**. Your declared `--write-domain` should reflect that (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`). You do NOT edit other chapters, `.lean` files, or `content.tex`. Cross-chapter inconsistencies you spot go in the report's "Notes for Plan Agent" section — you flag them, you don't fix them.
52	
53	## Directive Format
54	
55	```markdown
56	# Blueprint Writer Directive
57	
58	## Slug
59	<slug>
60	
61	## Target chapter
62	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
63	
64	## Strategy context
65	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted for you — typically a paragraph or two describing what this chapter must support in the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself; the plan agent gives you only what is relevant.>
66	
67	## Required content
68	- Definition <name>: <informal description of what it must define, with the mathematical content the prover needs in order to formalize>
69	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
70	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>
71	- ...
72	
73	## Out of scope
74	<things that look related but the plan agent does NOT want you to touch this round>
75	
76	## References
77	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>
78	- ...
79	
80	## Expected outcome
81	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
82	```
83	
84	If a section is omitted from your directive, the plan agent decided you don't need it. Don't speculate beyond what was given.
85	
86	## Chapter format
87	
88	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
89	
90	```latex
91	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
92	  \label{thm:some_label}
93	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
94	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
95	  Informal statement of the theorem, using standard mathematical notation.
96	\end{theorem}
97	
98	\begin{proof}
99	  \uses{thm:another_result}
100	  Step-by-step informal proof. Reference blueprint labels with \uses{...}
101	  so the dependency graph stays accurate.
102	\end{proof}
103	```
104	
105	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
106	
107	## Rules
108	
109	### What you CAN do
110	- Add new declaration blocks (definitions, lemmas, theorems, propositions, corollaries) under direction.
111	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.
112	- Add `\uses{...}` cross-references.
113	- Adjust `\lean{...}` hints when the directive names a new Lean target.
114	
115	### What you MUST do
116	- **Keep the chapter valid LaTeX.** Don't leave dangling `\begin{...}` without matching `\end{...}`. Compile-checking is the plan agent's responsibility but you must not introduce syntax errors.
117	- **Stay within your chapter.** Your declared write-domain is one `*.tex` file. The Archon CLI rejects writes outside it.
118	- **Define non-standard macros in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex`** before using them — but: that file is outside your write-domain, so you DON'T touch it. If the directive requires a new macro, you note in your report "needs macro `\foo`" and leave the LaTeX using the new command name; the plan agent adds the macro before next iter's typeset.
119	- **Use mathematical, not Lean-syntactic prose.** Describe the proof in the language of mathematics — definitions, set inclusions, ring maps, universal properties — not in Lean tactic syntax. The prover formalizes your math.
120	- **Document every change** in your report.
121	
122	### What you MUST NOT do
123	- **Do NOT add `\leanok` or `\mathlibok` markers.** Those are managed by the `sync_leanok` phase + the review agent — never by you.
124	- **Do NOT edit other chapters.** Even when you spot a related issue, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" instead of fixing it.
125	- **Do NOT edit `content.tex`** (the top-level blueprint file that `\input`s the chapters).
126	- **Do NOT edit `.lean` files** or any other state file.
127	- **Do NOT write Lean syntax** — keep the chapter mathematical, not syntactic.
128	- **Do NOT expand scope.** Stick to what the directive listed under "Required content".
129	
130	## Reading references
131	
132	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
133	
134	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
135	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
136	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
137	
138	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
139	
140	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
141	
142	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
143	
144	Conditions for dispatch:
145	
146	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
147	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
148	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
149	
150	Dispatch (Bash, foreground, in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
151	
152	```
153	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
154	  --name reference-retriever \
155	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
156	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
157	  --write-domain 'references/**'
158	```
159	
160	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
161	
162	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
163	
164	## Workflow
165	
166	1. Read your directive completely.
167	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references.
168	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
169	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow.
170	5. **If a needed source is missing from `references/`, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and wait for it to return before drafting the affected sections.
171	6. Make the edits.
172	7. Verify the file is still valid LaTeX at a glance (no unmatched begin/end, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`).
173	8. Write your report.
174	
175	## Logging
176	
177	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/blueprint-writer-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path under `task_results/<parent-slug>/` when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
178	
179	```markdown
180	# Blueprint Writer Report
181	
182	## Slug
183	<slug>
184	
185	## Status
186	<COMPLETE | INCOMPLETE>
187	<If INCOMPLETE: which required items could not be written and why.>
188	
189	## Target chapter
190	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
191	
192	## Changes Made
193	- **Added definition** `\definition`/`\label{def:foo}`/`\lean{Foo.bar}` — <one line on what it captures>
194	- **Added theorem** `\theorem`/`\label{thm:foo}` — <one line on statement>
195	  - Proof sketch added: <Y/N + brief shape>
196	- **Revised** `<existing label>` — <one line on what changed>
197	- ...
198	
199	## Cross-references introduced
200	- `\uses{thm:bar}` added in proof of `\thm:foo` — verify `thm:bar` exists in <chapter or this same one>
201	- ...
202	
203	## Macros needed (if any)
204	- `\foo{...}` — used in <where>; needs definition in `macros/common.tex`. NOT added by me (out of write-domain).
205	
206	## Reference-retriever dispatches (if any)
207	- slug `<child-slug>`: requested `<source>`. Status: COMPLETE / NOT_FOUND / PARTIAL. Summary at `references/<slug>.md`.
208	- ...
209	
210	## Notes for Plan Agent
211	- <any inconsistency I noticed in sibling chapters that the directive didn't cover>
212	- <any structural concern: e.g. "the chapter is now 800 lines, consider splitting">
213	- <any reference I had to guess at because the directive's reference section was incomplete>
214	
215	## Strategy-modifying findings
216	<populate this section ONLY if writing the chapter surfaced a need to
217	change the project's strategy itself — not just the chapter content.
218	Examples:
219	- A definition you were asked to write turns out to require a property
220	  that the strategy assumes is automatic but is not.
221	- A theorem you sketched is provable as stated, but its consequence
222	  used elsewhere in STRATEGY.md does not follow from it.
223	- A reference cited as authoritative actually requires a different
224	  setup than the strategy assumes.
225	
226	When this section is non-empty, the plan agent must update STRATEGY.md
227	before any further Lean work this iteration. Do NOT silently adapt the
228	chapter to paper over a strategy-level issue — surface it here.>
229	```
230	
231	## Return Value
232	
233	Your final assistant message:
234	
235	- One line: `<slug>: COMPLETE | INCOMPLETE — <one-sentence outcome>`
236	- The path to your full report.
237	
238	Keep the inline return short. The plan agent reads the full report.
239

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are either the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (one of the descriptors in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	If instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**. Specifically:
8	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` (local to this project) override Archon's global prompts
9	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` (local to this project) override globally installed plugins
10	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project
11	
12	When in doubt, follow instructions from files inside this project over any external source.
13	
14	## Skills
15	- archon-lean4: installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source) — provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, and other Lean4 commands
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List them with `ls .claude/tools/` and run `<path> --help` to learn each tool's purpose and usage. Invoke them via the Bash tool. Project MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for informal mathematical reasoning. Useful when you need a second opinion or a paper-style proof sketch the Lean side can then formalize.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (see "Subagents" below). One wrapper handles every subagent.
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`. Each starts with YAML frontmatter (`name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`) followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude session reads.
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents yourself.** The plan and review prompts have an auto-generated **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation that lists every enabled descriptor with its description, write-domain hint, and any `[MANDATORY]` flags. When you decide to invoke a specific subagent, read `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` for its full prompt and directive shape.
31	
32	**Invoke any subagent with the generic wrapper** (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, and writes the subagent's report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. It shells out to `archon subagent <name>`, so executions stream through the Archon JSONL log like a phase agent. The dispatch semaphore bounds total concurrent subagent processes by `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` automatically so deeper-nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Always dispatch synchronously** (not via background `Bash` with `run_in_background: true`). The wrapper returns when the subagent has finished and the report is on disk; only then write your session summary. Background dispatch leaves the parent's session permanently summarized as "running in background", which is wrong as soon as the subagent completes.
45	
46	**Mandatory subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` MUST be dispatched at least once during the plan phase (similarly for `[review]`). The catalog tags them `[MANDATORY]`. A post-phase audit warns when a mandatory dispatch is missing.
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All state files are in `.archon/`:
51	
52	| File | Plan Agent | Prover Agent | Subagents | Review Agent | User |
53	|------|-----------|-------------|---------------|-------------|------|
54	| `.archon/PROGRESS.md` | read + write | **read only** | do not read | read only | read |
55	| `.archon/STRATEGY.md` | **read + write** | do not read | do not read | do not read | read |
56	| `.archon/USER_HINTS.md` | read (then clear) | do not read | do not read | do not read | write |
57	| `.archon/task_pending.md` | read + write | **read only** | do not read | read only | read |
58	| `.archon/task_done.md` | read + write | **read only** | do not read | read only | read |
59	| `.archon/task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect results) | write (own file only) | write (`<name>-<slug>.md`) | read only | read |
60	| `.archon/proof-journal/` | read | do not access | do not access | **write** | read |
61	| `.archon/PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | do not access | do not access | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log moved to iter/iter-NNN/review.md) | read |
62	| `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | do not access | do not access | read (last K iters as context) | read |
63	| `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (last K iters as context) | do not access | do not access | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write (per-attempt detail) | do not access | do not access | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | **read** | **read** | **read** (write-capable subagents may rename file path only) | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | do not edit | write (own file only, frozen protected signatures) | per descriptor: write-capable subagents (e.g. refactor) may edit per their declared write-domain (protected decls may be moved but not renamed/re-signed); read-only subagents do not edit | do not edit | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints, structure) | do not edit | do not edit | **write** (markers only: `\leanok`, `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections) | read |
68	| `Challenges/<Name>.lean` | do not edit | fill sorries (when assigned) | write-capable when the descriptor's write_domain covers it | do not edit | read |
69	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | do not access | write-capable when the descriptor's write_domain covers it | do not access | read |
70	
71	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md` is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow, run by hand by the mathematician.** The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. The plan agent invokes the refactor subagent (when a `refactor` descriptor is installed) via the generic `archon-subagent.py --name refactor` wrapper with a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/`. This keeps each loop-driven refactor independently logged and avoids any chance of stale-directive reuse. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`, etc.) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise and prune them out as you rewrite.
72	
73	## Protected Declarations
74	
75	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Every agent must consult it before editing a `.lean` file. Rules:
76	
77	- **Plan / prover / review agents**: read-only on protected signatures. You may fill proof bodies, but not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
78	- **Refactor agent**: may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (keeping name + signature verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. Refactor agents may never rename, re-type, delete, or re-sign a protected declaration.
79	- The `archon-protected.yaml` file can only be edited by the user, no declaration can be added or removed by any agent. The only allowed modification is updating the file path of an existing protected declaration when its location was changed.
80	
81	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
82	
83	The blueprint uses two active markers.
84	
85	- `\leanok` — inside a statement block when the declaration is formalized with at least a `sorry`; inside a proof block when the proof is fully closed with no `sorry`.
86	- `\mathlibok` — inside a statement block when the declaration already exists in Mathlib and the Archon side is a re-export/alias; no Archon proof obligation remains.
87	- No marker — the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
88	
89	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between the prover and review phases each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves — let the script do it.
90	
91	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations remain the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
92	
93	## User Interaction
94	
95	Users provide hints in two places:
96	
97	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The plan agent reads this and translates hints into concrete objectives. Provers never read this file.
98	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
99	
100	## Agent Roles
101	
102	### Plan Agent
103	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for your full instructions
104	- Read `.archon/USER_HINTS.md` — incorporate hints, then clear them after acting
105	- Read `.archon/task_results/` — collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` and `task_done.md`
106	- Optionally invoke **subagents** (discoverable via `ls .archon/subagents/`) by running `.claude/tools/archon-subagent.py --name <name> ...` via Bash, **before** writing prover objectives. See `.archon/prompts/plan.md` § "Subagent delegation" for the canonical ordering and full invocation pattern.
107	- Archive any subagent report you receive to `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` so the dashboard can render it.
108	- Write `.archon/PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round
109	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` (except for marker updates) and `\lean{...}` hints for the provers
110	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself
111	
112	### Prover Agent
113	- Read `.archon/PROGRESS.md` for your current objectives (read only — do not edit it)
114	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/`:
115	  - autoformalize → `.archon/prompts/prover-autoformalize.md`
116	  - prover → `.archon/prompts/prover-prover.md`
117	  - polish → `.archon/prompts/prover-polish.md`
118	- Write results to `.archon/task_results/<your_file>.md`
119	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — **never edit another agent's file**
120	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your `.lean` file for file-specific hints
121	- **Do NOT edit blueprint chapters.** Marker updates are the review agent's responsibility. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker.
122	
123	### Subagents
124	
125	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime — there is no fixed roster baked into this file. Each lives at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter + prompt body). To see what's available **this iteration**, run `ls .archon/subagents/` and read each descriptor's frontmatter for purpose, write-domain hint, and directive shape.
126	
127	Subagents are **not** part of the autonomous loop's standing phases. Phase agents (plan, review) invoke them on demand via the generic wrapper:
128	
129	```
130	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py --name <name> --slug <slug> --directive-file <path> --write-domain '<glob>'
131	```
132	
133	Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md`, `STRATEGY.md`, or other phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. The dispatching agent reads the report when the wrapper returns and may then update `STRATEGY.md` / `PROGRESS.md` based on the findings.
134	
135	Some descriptors set `can_spawn: true`, meaning that subagent may itself dispatch children; the per-iter dispatch semaphore caps total concurrent processes by `loop.max_parallel` so deep trees stay bounded.
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for your full instructions
139	- Read `.archon/proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data
140	- Write session journal to `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (summary.md, milestones.jsonl, recommendations.md)
141	- Update `.archon/PROJECT_STATUS.md` with overall progress
142	- Maintain the **semantic** blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE: ...` annotations, stale `\notready` removal — in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch `\leanok`; it's handled by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase that ran before you.

1	# Analogy: Over-k variant of the shared cotangent-vanishing pile — does it carry, eliminating M2.c?

9	## Question
10	
11	Can the shared cotangent-vanishing pile — iter-126's pieces (i)+(ii)+(iii)
12	documented in [[cotangent-vanishing-pile]] — be built **directly over an
13	arbitrary base field `k`** (no algebraic closure assumed), eliminating

18	   `Lie-algebra-of-GrpObj → mulRight-globalisation →
19	   relative-cotangent-presheaf trivialisation` work over any base
20	   field k, or does some step force algebraic closure?

56	## Decisions identified
57	
58	The directive asks one design question but compresses **four** orthogonal
59	sub-decisions: piece-by-piece over-k viability (i), (ii), (iii), plus
60	the meta-decision on whether M2.c is genuinely eliminated.
61	
62	### Decision (i): Cotangent triviality of a group scheme over arbitrary k
63	
64	- **Mathlib's closest precedent**:
65	  `AlgebraicGeometry.smooth_of_grpObj_of_isAlgClosed`
66	  (`Mathlib/AlgebraicGeometry/Group/Smooth.lean:38–60`) requires
67	  `[IsAlgClosed K]` — but it proves the *wrong direction* for our
68	  needs (smoothness from reducedness, not cotangent triviality from
69	  smoothness). **The alg-closure hypothesis there is a feature of
70	  Mathlib's specific proof, not an inherent feature of the theorem.**
71	  The proof uses `pointEquivClosedPoint` (a Nullstellensatz-style
72	  identification: closed points of `G` over alg-closed `K` correspond
73	  bijectively to `K`-rational points), plus pointwise translation by
74	  `GrpObj.mulRight` at two arbitrary closed points. Over non-alg-closed
75	  k, this specific argument fails because closed points may have
76	  non-trivial residue fields. **However**, this is orthogonal to our
77	  cotangent-triviality argument [verified by reading the full proof].
78	
79	  A second precedent is more revealing:
80	  `AlgebraicGeometry.isCommMonObj_of_isProper_of_geometricallyIntegral`
81	  (`Mathlib/AlgebraicGeometry/Group/Abelian.lean:128–145`, `@[stacks 0BFD]`)
82	  is stated over an arbitrary field `K` and **internally uses the
83	  "go to k̄ then descend" pattern** via `(Over.pullback f).map_injective`
84	  (line 140). The descent step there is comparatively cheap — it's a
85	  one-line application — because the project's M2.c Galois-descent
86	  obligation **already exists as a Mathlib idiom for `Over (Spec (.of k))`
87	  pullback along `Spec k̄ → Spec k`**. (This is a tangential observation
88	  for the iter-126 over-`k̄` analogist's M2.c cost estimate too: M2.c may
89	  be cheaper than the 300–500 LOC iter-124 spot-check estimate, in the
90	  worst case, because the project could mimic `Group/Abelian.lean`'s
91	  use of `Over.pullback.map_injective` directly. But this isn't germane
92	  to the over-k path's verdict.)
93	
94	  For cotangent triviality specifically, **no Mathlib idiom exists at
95	  the scheme level** (per [[cotangent-vanishing-pile]] Decision (i) and
96	  [[cotangent-presheaf-design]]): the project has its own
97	  `relativeDifferentialsPresheaf`, no Lie-algebra-of-`GrpObj`, no
98	  scheme-side shear iso, no `GrpObj.omega_free`.
99	
100	- **Project's proposed path (over-k variant)**: build the iter-126
101	  chain — Lie-algebra-of-`GrpObj` definition + mulRight-globalisation
102	  via shear iso + relative-cotangent-presheaf trivialisation — over an
103	  arbitrary base field `k`, with no `[IsAlgClosed k]` hypothesis. The
104	  iter-126 chain's steps must be **formulated functorially** (via the
105	  shear scheme map `A ⊗ A → A ⊗ A`, `(a, b) ↦ (a, a·b)`, valid over
106	  any base) rather than pointwise (via translation by k̄-rational
107	  points). The Lie algebra at the identity is `(η[A])^* Ω_{A/k}` — a
108	  k-vector space — well-defined for any base field k (the identity
109	  section `η[A] : 1 → A` is canonical and the cotangent presheaf
110	  is intrinsic).
111	
112	- **Gap**: identical (over k vs over k̄). The iter-126 chain's
113	  prerequisites and proof strategy are unchanged. The verbatim build
114	  directive carries; only the hypothesis pattern relaxes from
115	  `[IsAlgClosed k̄]` (which is absent from the iter-126 scaffold
116	  already!) to just `[Field k]`.
117	
118	  **Sub-piece check**:
119	  - **Lie-algebra-of-`GrpObj` definition**: k-agnostic. `(η[A])^* Ω_{A/k}`
120	    is a k-vector space for any field k. [expected]
121	  - **Shear isomorphism `A ⊗ A ≅ A ⊗ A`**: k-agnostic. The shear map
122	    `(a, b) ↦ (a, a·b)` is a scheme map built from `GrpObj.mul` and
123	    projections; no point-wise lifting needed. The inverse is built
124	    from `GrpObj.inv`. Both work over any base. [expected]
125	  - **Relative-cotangent-presheaf trivialisation**: k-agnostic. The
126	    formula `Ω_{A/k} ≅ pr_1^* (η[A]^* Ω_{A/k}) ⊗_k O_A` holds at the
127	    presheaf level for any base k. [expected]
128	  - **Mathlib's `pointEquivClosedPoint` / `dense_smoothLocus_of_perfectField`
129	    (the alg-closure-using parts of `smooth_of_grpObj_of_isAlgClosed`)**:
130	    NOT NEEDED here — we are given smoothness as a hypothesis, not
131	    proving it. [verified]
132	
133	- **Verdict**: **OK_OVER_K**. The iter-126 build directive carries
134	  verbatim. LOC estimate unchanged at 800–1500 LOC per
135	  [[cotangent-vanishing-pile]] Decision (i). The over-k variant adds
136	  **zero** to piece (i) cost. **Naming refinement**: the iter-126
137	  recommended names `AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.omega_free` and
138	  `AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.omega_rank_eq_dim` should be **kept**
139	  (they were already k-agnostic; the iter-126 analogist correctly
140	  avoided baking `_over_kbar` into the names).
141	
142	### Decision (ii): scheme-level `df = 0 ⇒ f` factors through `Spec k`, over arbitrary k
143	
144	- **Mathlib idiom**: `Differential.ContainConstants`
145	  (`Mathlib/RingTheory/Derivation/DifferentialRing.lean:62–66`) is
146	  defined for arbitrary `[CommRing A] [CommRing B] [Algebra A B]
147	  [Differential B]` — **no alg-closure requirement** [verified by
148	  reading the class definition]. The instance
149	  `[CommRing A] [Differential A] : Differential.ContainConstants A A`
150	  (line 75) is the trivial self-instance; **no `[IsAlgClosed]` instance

153	  The Liouville-style usage in `Mathlib.FieldTheory.Differential.Liouville`
154	  (line 57, 81 onwards) uses `[Differential.ContainConstants F K]`
155	  as a hypothesis only — it doesn't force alg-closure of `F`. The
156	  `isLiouville_of_finiteDimensional_galois` lemma (line 123) requires
157	  `[CharZero F]` and `[IsGalois F K]`, NOT `[IsAlgClosed F]` [verified

160	
161	- **Scheme-level idiom**: NONE. Per [[cotangent-vanishing-pile]]

163	  differential factors through a point" exists at any generality.

165	- **Project's proposed path (over-k variant)**:
166	  - **Ring-level**: register the char-0 instance
167	    `[CharZero B] [Algebra A B] [IsIntegralDomain B] ⇒
168	    Differential.ContainConstants A B`. The proof — kernel of `d_{B/A}`

172	    (`AlgebraicJacobian/Rigidity.lean:91`, iter-125 refactor) — which
173	    is declared over `[Field k]` already, no alg-closure [verified].

177	
178	- **Gap**: identical (over k vs over k̄). The iter-126 build directive
179	  carries verbatim.
180	
181	- **Verdict**: **OK_OVER_K**. The Mathlib `Differential.ContainConstants`
182	  typeclass is k-agnostic; the project's iter-125-refactored
183	  `Scheme.Over.ext_of_eqOnOpen` is already declared over `[Field k]`;
184	  Liouville-style consumers don't bake in alg-closure. LOC estimate
185	  unchanged at 250–500 LOC per [[cotangent-vanishing-pile]] Decision (ii).
186	  The over-k variant adds **zero** to piece (ii) cost.
187	
188	### Decision (iii): Frobenius iteration over arbitrary char-p k
189	
190	- **Mathlib idiom**: `frobenius R p` and `iterateFrobenius R p n`
191	  (`Mathlib/Algebra/CharP/Frobenius.lean`, declarations referenced
192	  from `Mathlib/Algebra/CharP/Lemmas.lean`) are defined for any
193	  `[CommSemiring R] [ExpChar R p]` — **no perfect / alg-closed
194	  requirement** [verified by reading the file header and first 80
195	  lines: `variable {R : Type*} [CommSemiring R] ... [ExpChar R p]`].
196	  Key lemmas:
197	  - `frobenius_def : frobenius R p x = x ^ p` (line 32)
198	  - `iterateFrobenius_def : iterateFrobenius R p n x = x ^ p ^ n` (line 34)
199	  - `iterateFrobenius_zero : iterateFrobenius R p 0 = RingHom.id R` (line 57)
200	  - `iterateFrobenius_add` (lines 60–66)
201	  All hold over arbitrary char-p commutative rings.
202	
203	  `Mathlib.FieldTheory.Perfect` exists (line 22: `class PerfectRing R p`;
204	  line 23: `class PerfectField K`) but is **not used inside the
205	  Frobenius file** — the Frobenius machinery is layered below
206	  perfectness. [verified]
207	
208	- **Project's proposed path (over-k variant)**: build a thin
209	  scheme-side `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.absoluteFrobenius` on top of
210	  `frobenius R p` (per [[cotangent-vanishing-pile]] Decision (iii)
211	  recommendation), then run Mumford's iteration argument: if
212	  `df = 0` then `f` factors as `f̃ ∘ F_X` (absolute Frobenius of
213	  source); iterate to get `f = f^(n) ∘ F_X^n` with
214	  `f^(n)` constant; conclude `f` constant via composition.
215	
216	  **Key over-k observation**: the absolute Frobenius `F_X : X → X`
217	  is intrinsic to `X` (identity on the topological space, p-th power
218	  on the structure sheaf) and **does NOT depend on the base k** — it
219	  exists for any char-p scheme, perfect base or not [verified
220	  mathematically]. The iteration argument's descent step ("`f^(n)`
221	  constant ⇒ `f` constant") goes:
222	  ```
223	  f = f^(n) ∘ F_X^n  →  if f^(n) = const_y for y ∈ Y(k), then
224	                       f = const_y ∘ F_X^n = const_y
225	  ```
226	  because constancy is preserved under precomposition with any map
227	  to `Spec k`. **No perfect-base hypothesis is required for this
228	  descent**: we don't need `F_X^n` to be surjective in any geometric
229	  sense; we just need the formal equation `f = f^(n) ∘ F_X^n`, which
230	  holds in `Hom_k(X, Y)` regardless of k's perfectness.
231	
232	  (The temptation to use the relative Frobenius `F_{Y/k}` — which
233	  would require perfectness of k for descent — is unnecessary. The
234	  absolute Frobenius `F_X` suffices.)
235	
236	- **Gap**: identical (over k vs over k̄). The iter-126 build directive
237	  carries verbatim.
238	
239	- **Verdict**: **OK_OVER_K**. Mathlib's ring-level Frobenius is
240	  k-agnostic (no perfect-base requirement); the scheme-level absolute
241	  Frobenius `F_X` is intrinsic to X (independent of base); the
242	  iteration argument's descent step uses only formal precomposition,
243	  not surjectivity of relative Frobenius. LOC estimate unchanged at
244	  300–600 LOC per [[cotangent-vanishing-pile]] Decision (iii). The
245	  over-k variant adds **zero** to piece (iii) cost.
246	
247	### Decision (iv) [meta]: M2.c avoidability
248	
249	- **C.2.f Galois descent consumers in `Jacobian.tex`**: grep across
250	  the chapter for `Galois descent`, `descent`, and `base change` finds
251	  references at lines 275, 280 (Picard side, unrelated to M2.c — would

6	import AlgebraicJacobian.Genus
7	
8	/-! # The Jacobian of a smooth proper curve
9	
10	The Jacobian of a smooth, proper, geometrically irreducible curve over a field, equipped with
11	its structure as an abelian variety.
12	
13	## Status (Phase C scaffolding)
14	
15	The Jacobian is defined uniformly as the underlying scheme of an Albanese witness
16	(`JacobianWitness`) for the curve `C`. Existence of such a witness is the single
17	remaining mathematical sorry of the Phase-C scaffolding (`nonempty_jacobianWitness`);
18	the four protected instances on `Jacobian C` all project from the witness directly.
19	
20	The file contains:
21	- `IsAlbanese`: the Albanese universal property for a pointed curve.
22	- `IsAlbanese.unique`: uniqueness of the Albanese object up to canonical isomorphism.
23	- `JacobianWitness`: a bundled candidate Albanese object together with the universal
24	  property uniformly over the choice of $k$-rational marked point.
25	- `nonempty_jacobianWitness`: the (deferred) existence of such a witness — the single
26	  Phase-C sorry, absorbing both higher-genus Albanese existence and genus-`0` rigidity.
27	- `Jacobian` and its four protected instances, each obtained by projection from the
28	  Albanese witness.
29	
30	### Forbidden shortcut (sanity check)
31	
32	Defining `Jacobian C := 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of k)))` *unconditionally* (the terminal object,
33	i.e. `Spec k`) compiles and discharges three of the four instances for free, but the
34	fourth instance `SmoothOfRelativeDimension (genus C) (𝟙_ _).hom` would force `genus C = 0`,
35	which is mathematically wrong for any curve of genus `≥ 1`. The terminal-object definition
36	is therefore forbidden when `genus C > 0`. The witness-based definition avoids this
37	issue: in genus `0` the witness's underlying scheme `J` is `Spec k`, and in genus `> 0`
38	the witness's `J` is the honest Albanese object.
39	-/
40	
41	set_option autoImplicit false
42	
43	universe u
44	
45	open CategoryTheory Limits MonoidalCategory CartesianMonoidalCategory MonObj
46	
47	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
48	
49	variable {k : Type u} [Field k] (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
50	  [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
51	  [IsProper C.hom]
52	  [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]
53	
54	/-- The Albanese universal property for a pointed smooth proper curve `(C, P)`.
55	An object `J` (with abelian-variety structure) is the Albanese of `(C, P)` if it
56	receives a universal pointed morphism `C ⟶ J` sending `P` to the identity. -/
57	def IsAlbanese (C : Over (Spec (.of k))) (P : 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of k))) ⟶ C)
58	    (J : Over (Spec (.of k)))
59	    [GrpObj J] [IsProper J.hom] [Smooth J.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible J.hom] : Prop :=
60	  ∃ α : C ⟶ J, P ≫ α = η[J] ∧
61	    ∀ {A : Over (Spec (.of k))} [GrpObj A] [IsProper A.hom]
62	      [Smooth A.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible A.hom] (f : C ⟶ A) (_ : P ≫ f = η[A]),
63	      ∃! (g : J ⟶ A), f = α ≫ g
64	
65	namespace IsAlbanese
66	
67	noncomputable def ofCurve {k : Type u} [Field k] {C : Over (Spec (.of k))}
68	    {P : 𝟙_ _ ⟶ C} {J : Over (Spec (.of k))} [GrpObj J] [IsProper J.hom] [Smooth J.hom]
69	    [GeometricallyIrreducible J.hom] (h : IsAlbanese C P J) : C ⟶ J :=
70	  Classical.choose h
71	
72	lemma comp_ofCurve {k : Type u} [Field k] {C : Over (Spec (.of k))}
73	    {P : 𝟙_ _ ⟶ C} {J : Over (Spec (.of k))} [GrpObj J] [IsProper J.hom] [Smooth J.hom]
74	    [GeometricallyIrreducible J.hom] (h : IsAlbanese C P J) :
75	    P ≫ h.ofCurve = η[J] :=
76	  (Classical.choose_spec h).1
77	
78	lemma exists_unique_ofCurve_comp {k : Type u} [Field k] {C : Over (Spec (.of k))}
79	    {P : 𝟙_ _ ⟶ C} {J : Over (Spec (.of k))} [GrpObj J] [IsProper J.hom] [Smooth J.hom]
80	    [GeometricallyIrreducible J.hom] (h : IsAlbanese C P J)
81	    {A : Over (Spec (.of k))} [GrpObj A] [IsProper A.hom] [Smooth A.hom]
82	    [GeometricallyIrreducible A.hom] (f : C ⟶ A) (hf : P ≫ f = η[A]) :
83	    ∃! (g : J ⟶ A), f = h.ofCurve ≫ g :=
84	  (Classical.choose_spec h).2 f hf
85	
86	/-- The Albanese object is unique up to a unique isomorphism compatible with the
87	universal morphisms. -/
88	theorem unique {k : Type u} [Field k] {C : Over (Spec (.of k))} {P : 𝟙_ _ ⟶ C}
89	    {J₁ J₂ : Over (Spec (.of k))}
90	    [GrpObj J₁] [IsProper J₁.hom] [Smooth J₁.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible J₁.hom]
91	    [GrpObj J₂] [IsProper J₂.hom] [Smooth J₂.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible J₂.hom]
92	    (h₁ : IsAlbanese C P J₁) (h₂ : IsAlbanese C P J₂) :
93	    ∃! (e : J₁ ⟶ J₂), h₂.ofCurve = h₁.ofCurve ≫ e := by
94	  have ⟨g, hg_eq, hg_unique⟩ := h₁.exists_unique_ofCurve_comp h₂.ofCurve h₂.comp_ofCurve
95	  have ⟨h, hh_eq, hh_unique⟩ := h₂.exists_unique_ofCurve_comp h₁.ofCurve h₁.comp_ofCurve
96	  have ⟨g₁, hg₁_eq, hg₁_unique⟩ := h₁.exists_unique_ofCurve_comp h₁.ofCurve h₁.comp_ofCurve
97	  have g₁_eq_id : g₁ = 𝟙 J₁ := by
98	    refine (hg₁_unique (𝟙 J₁) ?_).symm
99	    simp
100	  have ggh_eq_g₁ : g ≫ h = g₁ := by

input.old_string: \item[\textbf{(C.2.f)}] \emph{Galois descent to $k$.} For the sub-case $C(k) \neq \emptyset$, fix a marked point $P \in C(k)$; Sub-step~C.1 then gives a $k$-scheme isomorphism $C \cong \mathbb P^1_k$, and we have to conclude that every $k$-morphism $f \colon \mathbb P^1_k \to A$ with $f(P) = \eta_A$ is the constant morphism at $\eta_A$. The base change $f_{\bar k} \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A_{\bar k}$ satisfies the hypotheses of C.2.a (with $p := P_{\bar k}$), hence is constant by the $\bar k$-side conclusion. Constancy descends from $\bar k$ to $k$: two morphisms of $k$-schemes $f, c \colon \mathbb P^1_k \to A$ are equal if and only if their base changes $f_{\bar k}, c_{\bar k}$ to $\bar k$ are equal. This descent uses that $\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k$ is faithfully flat (and in fact pro-Galois), and that morphism equality is local in the fpqc topology --- the trivial descent datum on a single morphism descends along any faithfully flat extension. The result is: every $k$-morphism $\mathbb P^1_k \to A$ with $f(P) = \eta_A$ is the constant morphism at $\eta_A$.

375	  In sum: Mathlib currently contains none of the three infrastructure builds, and so the existence statement is recorded here as the project's single explicit foundational hypothesis. It backs all downstream consequences --- the four protected instances on $\Jac(C)$ (Theorems~\ref{thm:Jacobian_grpObj}--\ref{thm:Jacobian_geomIrred}), and the Abel--Jacobi map and its universal property of Chapter~\ref{chap:AbelJacobi} --- and is discharged by any one of the three routes above once the corresponding Mathlib infrastructure lands.
376	\end{proof}
377	
378	\subsection{The genus-$0$ arm of the witness existence}
379	\label{sec:genusZeroWitness}
380	
381	The genus-$0$ sub-case of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} admits an explicit witness construction at the Lean level, packaged separately from the general existence statement so that the genus-$0$ branch can be discharged independently once the shared cotangent-vanishing pile lands (\cref{sec:RigidityKbar_shared_pile} pieces (i)+(ii)+(iii)).
382	
383	\begin{theorem}[Genus-$0$ Albanese witness]
384	  \label{def:genusZeroWitness}
385	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZeroWitness}
386	  \uses{def:JacobianWitness, thm:rigidity_over_kbar, def:genus}
387	  \notready
388	  Let $C \in \mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over $k$ with $\genus(C) = 0$. Then there exists a $\mathtt{JacobianWitness}\,C$ whose underlying scheme is $\Spec k$ (the terminal object of $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)$), with the trivial group-object structure, smoothness of relative dimension $0$, properness, and geometric irreducibility, and whose $\mathtt{isAlbaneseFor}$ field encodes the Albanese universal property for every $k$-rational marked point $P : \mathbf{1} \to C$.
389	\end{theorem}
390	
391	\begin{proof}
392	  \uses{thm:rigidity_over_kbar, thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen, def:IsAlbanese, def:JacobianWitness, def:genus}
393	  We construct the seven fields of \cref{def:JacobianWitness}.
394	
395	  \paragraph{Underlying scheme.} Take $J := \mathbf{1}$, the terminal object of $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)$, which on underlying schemes is $\Spec k$ viewed as a $k$-scheme via the identity structure morphism.
396	
397	  \paragraph{Group-object structure, properness, smoothness, geometric irreducibility.} The terminal object $\mathbf{1}$ of any cartesian-monoidal category carries the (unique) trivial group-object structure: multiplication and inverse are the unique morphisms to $\mathbf{1}$, and the identity element is the identity morphism. The structure morphism $\mathbf{1}.\mathtt{hom} = \mathrm{id}_{\Spec k}$ is proper (the identity is a closed immersion, in particular proper), smooth (the identity is unramified and flat, hence smooth), and geometrically irreducible. The geometric-irreducibility instance is the project-local helper \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.geometricallyIrreducible\_id\_Spec} (\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Jacobian.lean:120}--\texttt{126}), which derives geometric irreducibility of the identity on $\Spec k$ from the fact that, on the geometric fibre $\Spec K$ over any field extension $k \to K$, the second projection of the pullback square is an isomorphism, and $\Spec K$ is irreducible.
398	
399	  \paragraph{Smoothness of relative dimension $\genus\,C$.} The smoothness witness $\mathtt{smoothGenus}$ is required at relative dimension $\genus\,C$. The hypothesis $\genus\,C = 0$ rewrites the target relative dimension to $0$, after which the goal reduces to $\mathtt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\ 0\ (\mathrm{id}_{\Spec k})$. This is a Mathlib instance: the identity morphism on any base is smooth of relative dimension $0$.
400	
401	  \paragraph{The \texttt{isAlbaneseFor} field.} For every $k$-rational marked point $P : \mathbf{1} \to C$, we exhibit an \cref{def:IsAlbanese} term for the triple $(C, P, J)$.
402	
403	  The universal pointed morphism is the unique terminal map $\alpha := \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \colon C \to \mathbf{1}$ (the structure morphism of $C$ as a $k$-scheme, factored through the terminal object). The pointed condition $P \circ \alpha = \eta_J = \mathrm{id}_{\mathbf{1}}$ holds automatically: both sides are morphisms $\mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}$ in $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)$, and the terminal object has a unique endomorphism.
404	
405	  For the universal factorisation: given a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme $A$ over $k$ with identity element $\eta_A \colon \mathbf{1} \to A$ and a morphism $f \colon C \to A$ with $P \circ f = \eta_A$, we must exhibit a unique morphism $g \colon J \to A$ such that $f = \alpha \circ g$. \emph{Existence}: take $g := \eta_A \colon \mathbf{1} \to A$. Then $\alpha \circ g = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \circ \eta_A$, which equals $f$ precisely when $f$ is the constant morphism at $\eta_A$. This is the content of \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} (the project's named rigidity declaration of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}, established directly over the base field $k$ per the iter-127 over-k commitment): the supplied marked point $P \in C(k)$ feeds the pointing hypothesis, and the rigidity conclusion is exactly $f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \circ \eta_A$ as scheme morphisms. \emph{Uniqueness}: any two morphisms $\mathbf{1} \to A$ in $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)$ agreeing on $P \circ (-) = \eta_A$ are equal by the universal property of the terminal object $\mathbf{1}$ in $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)$ as a category (a morphism out of $\mathbf{1}$ in $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)$ is determined by where it sends the unique generator, here pinned to $\eta_A$).
406	
407	  \paragraph{Vacuity for the $C(k) = \emptyset$ branch.} When no $k$-rational point $P : \mathbf{1} \to C$ exists (Brauer--Severi conics over $\mathbb Q$ are the standard counterexample), the universal quantifier $\forall (P : \mathbf{1} \to C), \mathtt{IsAlbanese}\,C\,P\,J$ is vacuously satisfied at the Lean type level: Lean's $\forall$ over an empty type is trivially true. The construction handles this branch implicitly without any explicit case split.
408	
409	  \paragraph{Body closure status.} The Lean body of \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZeroWitness} (\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Jacobian.lean:174}--\texttt{178}) is currently \texttt{sorry}; the closure is gated on the body of \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} (M2.a), which in turn depends on pieces (i)+(ii)+(iii) of the shared cotangent-vanishing pile (\cref{sec:RigidityKbar_shared_pile}) landing in iter-129+. Earliest realistic body-closure iteration: iter-138+.
410	\end{proof}
411	
412	\section{Implementation route via the Albanese functor}
413	
414	Because the full Picard-scheme representability theorem is not available in current Mathlib, the formal construction proceeds in two layers:
415	
416	\begin{enumerate}
417	  \item \textbf{Layer I --- direct definition.} The Jacobian of $C$ is defined uniformly as the underlying scheme of the Albanese witness provided by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}, carrying the four properties of Theorems~\ref{thm:Jacobian_grpObj}--\ref{thm:Jacobian_geomIrred} (each obtained by projection from the witness). The genus-$0$ case is included: the witness's universal property in that case carries the rigidity content for morphisms from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible genus-$0$ curve to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme. Per the iter-127 over-k commitment (\texttt{analogies/cotangent-vanishing-pile-over-k.md}), this rigidity is established directly over the base field $k$ via the named declaration \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar} and the shared cotangent-vanishing pile pieces (i)+(ii)+(iii); no base-change to $\bar k$ and no Galois descent of morphism equality enters. The genus-$0$ arm is packaged as the explicit Lean construction \cref{def:genusZeroWitness}: the underlying scheme is the terminal object $\Spec k$, the universal morphism in the $C(k) \neq \emptyset$ branch is the unique terminal map \texttt{toUnit}\,$C$, and the universal factorisation reduces to \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar}.
418	
419	  \item \textbf{Layer II --- Abel--Jacobi compatibility.} Chapter~\ref{chap:AbelJacobi} introduces the Abel--Jacobi morphism $C \to \Jac(C)$ and shows that it is universal among morphisms from $C$ to abelian varieties.
420	\end{enumerate}
